This letter is addressed to believers who are strangers in every city or country where they live, and are scattered throughout the nations. These people should attribute their salvation to God’s electing love, the Son's redemption, and the Holy Spirit’s sanctification; and so give glory to one God in three Persons, into whose name they had been baptized. Hope, as the world understands it, refers only to an uncertain good, because all worldly hopes are unstable, built on shaky foundations. The world’s hopes of heaven are blind guesses. But the hope of those who belong to the living God is a living hope—not just in what it promises, but also in its effect. It brings comfort and strength in times of distress, enabling them to overcome all difficulties. Mercy is the source of this; great mercy and abundant mercy. This well-founded hope of salvation motivates active obedience within the believer’s soul. The basis for a Christian's joy is remembering the happiness that awaits them. It is imperishable, it will never fail, and it is an inheritance that cannot be spent. It is also pure and perfect—signifying its purity. And it does not fade; it is always pleasant, consistently itself. All possessions here are flawed and incomplete; something is always lacking. Even beautiful houses have worries surrounding them, and comfortable beds and full tables can be accompanied by sickness and discomfort. All possessions are tainted by sin, either in how they are obtained or how they are used. How easily we turn the things we own into opportunities for sin, thinking that there’s no enjoyment to be found unless we misuse them! Worldly possessions are uncertain and quickly disappear, like flowers and plants in a field. The most valuable things are those stored in the highest and best place—in heaven. Those whose hearts the Holy Spirit directs toward this inheritance are blessed. God not only gives his people grace but also preserves them for glory. Every believer always has something to rejoice about; it should be evident in their appearance and behavior. The Lord does not willingly cause suffering, yet his wise love sometimes appoints difficult trials to reveal people’s hearts and ultimately benefit them. Gold doesn't become purer through fire—it diminishes. But faith is strengthened and multiplied by troubles and afflictions. Gold will eventually perish and can only buy perishable things, while the trial of faith will be found to bring praise, honor, and glory. Let this help us accept present suffering. Seek to believe in Christ’s excellence and his love for you; this will ignite a fire in your heart that will inspire a loving sacrifice to him. God's glory and our happiness are so intertwined that if we sincerely seek one now, we will attain the other when our souls are no longer subject to evil. The certainty of this hope is like having already received it.

Jesus Christ was the main focus of the prophets’ studies. Their investigation into Christ’s suffering and the glory that would follow led them to a complete understanding of the gospel—the essence of which is that Jesus Christ was delivered for our offenses and raised again for our justification. God is more willing to meet our needs than to grant our requests. The teaching of the prophets and the teaching of the apostles are in perfect agreement, because they both come from the same Holy Spirit. The gospel is a ministry carried out by the Spirit; its success depends on his power and blessing. Let us then carefully study those Scriptures that contain the teachings about salvation.

Just as travelers, racers, warriors, and laborers put on loose clothing to be ready for their work, so let Christians prepare their minds and emotions. Be sober-minded, watchful against all spiritual dangers and enemies, and temperate in all behavior. Be sober-minded in your beliefs as well as in your actions, and humble in your assessment of yourselves. A strong and complete trust in God’s grace is consistent with making every effort in your duty. Holiness is the desire and responsibility of every Christian—in all situations, under all circumstances, and toward everyone. We must especially be vigilant and pray against the sins to which we are prone. The written word of God is the surest guide for a Christian's life, and this rule commands us to be holy in every way. God makes those whom he saves holy.

Confidence in God as a Father and reverence for him as a Judge go together; and regarding God always as a Judge makes him dear to us as a Father. If believers do wrong, God will correct them. Therefore, let Christians not doubt God’s faithfulness to his promises or give way to an overwhelming fear of his wrath, but instead let them respect his holiness. A bold profession of faith leaves one defenseless, and Satan can take them captive at will; a discouraged professor lacks the courage to use their advantages and is easily defeated. The price paid for humanity's redemption was Christ’s precious blood. Not only openly wicked behavior, but also unproductive conversation is highly dangerous, even if it is considered customary. It is foolish to resolve that you will live and die in a certain way simply because your ancestors did so. God had plans of special favor toward his people long before he revealed such grace to them. But the clarity of truth, the support of faith, and the power of ordinances are much greater now than they were before Christ came to earth. The comfort is that, through faith, we are united with Christ, and his present glory assures us that where he is, we will be also. The soul must be purified before it can relinquish its own desires and indulgences. And the word of God planted in the heart by the Holy Spirit is a means of spiritual life, stirring up our duty, working a complete change in our dispositions and affections until it leads to eternal life. In contrast with the excellence of the renewed spiritual man, as born again, observe the emptiness of the natural man. In his life and in his fall, he is like grass, like the flower of grass that quickly withers and dies. We should listen to, and thus receive and love, the holy, living word, risking everything rather than losing it; and we must reserve a place for it in our hearts as our only treasures here, and as a certain pledge of the treasure of glory laid up for believers in heaven.
